Were You Asked This?
Design a Distributed Cache System

Design a distributed caching system that can serve data with low latency across multiple geographic locations, handling cache misses, replication, and scaling to support billions of records.

Asked at:

Microsoft

Google

Meta

Amazon

+1 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

A distributed cache is an in-memory key-value service (think Redis or Memcached) that sits in front of your databases and APIs to serve data with microseconds-to-milliseconds latency. Users read and write simple records (often JSON or binary blobs) by key, with optional time-to-live (TTL), and the cache offloads the backend while keeping latency predictable.

Interviewers ask you to design a distributed cache because it bundles core distributed systems skills into one problem: sharding and client routing, consistency versus freshness, replication and failover, multi-region design, hot-key contention, and the realities of cache invalidation. You’re expected to reason about trade-offs (cache-aside vs write-through), manage thundering herds, and describe concrete mechanisms that keep the system fast and correct under failures at global scale.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to set, get, and delete key–value entries with optional per-key TTLs.

Users should be able to read data with consistently low latency from the nearest available cache node or region.

Users should be able to configure eviction behavior to stay within memory limits (for example, LRU or LFU by default).

Users should be able to invalidate cached data precisely (single key) or broadly (namespace/tag) when underlying data changes.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you shard and route keys to ensure even distribution and painless rebalancing as nodes join/leave?

How do you keep data correct and prevent thundering herds given 100 ms database latency on cache misses?

How would you design multi-region caching for 10 geographies to achieve low latency while keeping caches coherent?

How will you achieve high availability and fast failover without hurting write latency?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Scaling Reads

A distributed cache’s primary job is to absorb massive read traffic and deliver sub-millisecond to single-digit millisecond responses. Sharding, replication, client-side routing, and request coalescing are the core techniques to meet aggressive latency and availability SLOs.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Hot keys and synchronized expirations cause thundering herds and tail latency spikes. Single-flight on misses, TTL jitter, negative caching, and selective hot-key replication are essential to keep the system stable under bursty, uneven workloads.

Read More
Real-time Updates

To keep multi-region caches coherent, you need efficient, near–real time propagation of invalidations and updates. A pub/sub or event-stream approach ensures downstream caches react quickly to changes without heavy cross-region reads.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Redis

Redis is the de facto in-memory cache with built-in TTLs, eviction (LRU/LFU), clustering, replication, and pub/sub. Redis Cluster plus client-side consistent hashing is a standard, expected baseline for low-latency caches in interviews.

Read More
Kafka

Kafka provides durable, ordered topics that scale globally, making it ideal for propagating cache invalidations and update events across shards and regions with at-least-once delivery and replay for recovery.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper (or etcd/Consul) supplies reliable coordination for cluster membership, leader election, and shard metadata. It underpins automated failover and consistent configuration in a distributed cache fleet.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Distributed Cache

It is effectively the same problem: designing sharding, replication, eviction, persistence trade-offs, and client routing for an in-memory key-value store like Redis.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Both systems battle hot-key contention and strict latency budgets. Sharded counters, consistent hashing, and single-flight patterns apply directly.

Read
Practice
FB News Feed

A read-heavy product that relies heavily on caching, invalidations, and fan-out. Handling freshness, cache warm-up, and hot-key mitigation parallels multi-region cache design.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Modulo-based sharding tied to cluster size

hash(key) % N remaps almost all keys on scale events or node failure, causing massive cache churn, cold shards, and downtime. Consistent hashing with virtual nodes is the expected solution.

Hand-waving cache coherence and invalidation

Ignoring how entries are invalidated or refreshed leads to stale data and stampedes on expiration. You should specify write policy, request coalescing, TTL jitter, negative caching, and a concrete invalidation/pub-sub mechanism.

No replication and failover plan

Single-instance shards create single points of failure. Interviewers expect primary–replica, health checks, leader election, and how replicas catch up after failover with acceptable read-after-write behavior.

You have a global DB in 1 region. There is an internal service which is about to launch which will have service deployed per region and it wants to call an API to read data from your global DB. Data in your DB is key value. Design a system so that the internal service can call you from regional endpoints. You don't have the budget to create replicas of your DB per region and the data is inherently global, cannot be sharded across regions.

Just a one word question. Design a cache. We had to clarify all requirements surrounding this . It’s a global cache for a distributed database with 100 billion records and each cache holds key value records so based on that do the sizing , distribution , latencies , read heavy, etc. Discuss all issues like replication, crashing of cache servers, cache warm up , TTL etc proactively.
